title: Occupation of Drakan
content:
The Occupation of Drakan was the first major planetary campaign conducted by the early Solar Empire. While officially framed at the time as a "mercy mission" to save the pre-FTL Drak'vorr from extinction, it is now largely believed to have been a calculated act of biological warfare engineered by clandestine elements within the Imperial Military's high command.

Prelude and Landing

The occupation was precipitated by the Orchid Plague, a virulent respiratory pathogen that appeared on Drakan in late 2694 (24 IE). Records recovered by Princess Luna's post-Imperial government hint that the pathogen was a bio-weapon engineered by a radical faction of the Imperial High Command. However, there is still no concrete evidence that outright confirms the theory. Later actions taken by the High Command confirm some sort of conspiracy to annex Drakan, regardless of the initial motive.

Bio-Weapon Theory

It is known that the Imperial Military's advanced research wing—an organization that existed entirely without governmental oversight—had abducted a number of Drak'vorr in the years proceeding the occupation; and that every single one were deceased by 2694. Records also show that a cure for the Orchid Plague had been completed at least as soon as three months after the initial landing; almost a year and a half before it was officially announced to the population on Drakan. This has lead many to scholars to believe that the pathogen and the cure had been developed prior to the first military landing on Drakan.

Operation: Angel Descent

Empress Celestia, reportedly unaware of any conspiracy, was presented with a binary choice: allow a sentient species to perish or authorize a full-scale military intervention. Under immense pressure from both the propaganda-driven civilian base and military war-hawks, she signed the Righteous Savior Mandate in early 2696. The mandate gave legal backing to the military's stated goal of humanitarian aid; and Operation: Angel Descent was quickly set in motion.

Minutes before the first dropships entered the atmosphere, Imperial satellites positioned in low orbit deployed high-intensity electronic warfare suites. Every telegraph line, primitive radio frequency, and signal fire network on Drakan was jammed by EM interference or physically severed via surgical kinetic strikes. To the steam-and-iron-age Drak'vorr, the arrival of warp-capable dropships was perceived as a divine or terrifying event. Relying instead on slow, direct communications, Drak'vorr nation-states were effectively blind and deaf. Regional governors had no idea their national capitals had fallen until Imperial troops arrived at their doorsteps.

The Solar Empire did not land in the wilderness. They utilized high-precision atmospheric insertion to place Roc-Class Heavy Dropships directly into the palace squares and parliament grounds of the planet's twelve major powers. The sheer scale of Imperial technology—Aquila-II Battleships the size of towns hovering high above soot-stained industrial capitals—shattered the Drak'vorr's collective will. The first units to exit the ships were not heavy infantry, but biological hazard teams flanked by intimidating but non-aggressive security details. They claimed to be here to cure for the Orchid Plague. To the starving and dying Drak'vorr populace, the invaders appeared as literal saviors.

Dissolution of Drak'vorr Civilization

Before the Imperial arrival, Drakan was divided into a dozen primary empires and numerous other protectorates and nation-states, most notably the Kingdom of Kgres and the Republic of Grand Yvok. Within 48 hours of the landing, these entities ceased to exist as sovereign powers. Imperial officers presented the Drak'vorr monarchs and presidents with the Thraskir Accord, named for their home star. The terms were non-negotiable: the Drak'vorr's leaders would abdicate power immediately; all national militaries were to stand down and surrender their armories to Imperial "Peacekeepers."

The old borders—often based on centuries of Drak'vorr ethnic history—were erased. The planet was carved into six Administrative Sectors, each overseen by an Imperial Military Directors. Leaders who signed immediately were kept as "High Counselors" to provide a facade of continuity. Those who refused were labeled "Contagion Risks" and removed to "Quarantine Facilities" (from which none ever returned).

To ensure the Drak'vorr remained subservient, the Empire implemented a policy to stunt the growth of the species: industry and traditional knowledge were targeted by the new military administration. Drak'vorr coal mines and steel mills were seized. The local workers were taught to operate Imperial machinery, but were forbidden from learning the physics behind them. Science academies were closed and replaced with military-run schools, which taught Imperial dogma and basic technical literacy.

EB07 Antiviral Rollout

For the first twelve months of the occupation, the Imperial military deliberately withheld the mass-distribution of the EB07 Antiviral. Imperial authorities claimed that "atmospheric interference" and "genetic variances" among the Drak'vorr required extensive localized testing. In reality, this year was used to allow the plague to dismantle the remaining social fabric of the Drak'vorr nation-states. By the end of the occupation's first year, the Drak'vorr death toll reached into the millions. The traditional authorities were seen as powerless, while the Imperial treatment facilities were the only places on the planet where hope existed. This created a dependence that the Empire exploited to gain total civilian cooperation.

When the rollout finally began, it was not a free humanitarian gift; but instead as another measure of Imperial control. To receive the cure, every Drak'vorr was required to register at an official treatment center. Under the provisions of the Demographic Identification Act, they were assigned a digital identification code, their biometrics were scanned, and their ancestral lineage was recorded. This gave the Empire a perfect census of the population for future taxation and conscription.

The cure was administered in three stages over six months. If a region showed signs of dissent or unauthorized gatherings, the Empire would simply "delay" the shipment of the second or third dose due to "security concerns," effectively using the plague as a remote-controlled executioner.

By the middle of 2697, the plague had been effectively neutralized in the major urban sectors. The Drak'vorr experienced a brief, euphoric "Era of Gratitude," believing they had emerged on the other side of this crisis. During this window, the Empire aided in rebuilding Drak'vorr cities. However, they replaced traditional Drak'vorr wooden and stone structures with expressionless concrete. City centers and strategic zones were a particular focus of the Imperial "reconstruction" effort. Hospitals were built, but they were placed inside fortified military compounds. Schools were reopened, but the curriculum was now focused on Imperial dogma.

Drak'vorr Resistance

The narrative of the Imperial "mercy mission" finally began to fade around the midway through 2697. The first signal that the Empire had no intention of leaving was the construction of massive footings in the three largest Drak'vorr cities. These were foundations for massive spires that served as logistical and command hubs for the military. Near the end of the year, the Empire took it's most obvious act of aggression yet: a degree was issued claiming "reimbursment" for the cost of the plague cure. The decree demanded 30% of all iron, coal, and cobalt extracted from the planet be diverted to imperial stockpiles.

On the second anniversary of the landing, a coalition of Drak'vorr scholars and former statesmen presented the Petition of 113, thanking the Empire for the cure and politely requesting a timeline for their departure. The Imperial response was the immediate arrest of the petitioners on charges of "Inciting Instability".


"The realization didn't come with a bang, but with the sound of mortar being poured. We looked up and realized the skyships weren't leaving; they were building towers to stay."

- Basmer Mers Ggrege, Chronicles of the First Resistance

Qmoq Massacre

The transition into a decade of violence on Drakan can be traced to a single event in the city of Qmoq: one of the three sites of an Imperial Spire. A peaceful assembly of a coalition Drak'vorr labor unions, scholars, farmers, and former political leaders gathered to protest the jailing of the petitioners. The local Imperial commander, fearing the large crowd could soon become uncontrollable, ordered a violent dispersal of protestors, resulting in over three hundred Drak'vorr dead and countless more arrested by the end of day.

The Qmoq Massacre, as it soon became known as, was witnessed by members of an underground organization known as the Grey Scouts. Former members of Qmoq's army, the Scouts had hidden their primitive kinetic weaponry during the initial landing, and had spent most of the occupation stockpiling arms and explosives in secret caches. Within hours of the massacre, the first imperial garrison was overrun on the outskirts of the city; not with plasma weaponry, but by mining explosives and sheer physical fury.

Organized Insurgency

While a fuse had been lit in Qmoq, the transformation from civilian discontent to organized rebellion was not immediate. It required the formation of a planetary network capable of operating under the shadow of mass technological surveillance. To this end, the Drak'vorr Underground, as it has come to be known by historians, was organized out of a patchwork of revolutionary cells, government remnants, labor unions, and frontier clans.

Made up of such a variety of organizations, the Underground operated with a largely decentralized command structure. Literally operating from within Drakan's extensive underground system, the resistance hid in deep subterranean complexes and high-altitude plateaus where Imperial sensors were less effective.

Since the Empire monitored all electronic frequencies, the resistance reverted to physical courier networks. Designated runners, the fastest and most durable of those in the rebellion, traversed the planet's vast wilderness on foot or via primitive steam-locomotives in tunnels the Empire hadn't mapped. They used a system of coded whistles and mechanical mirrors that appeared to Imperial eyes and ears as environmental reflections or wildlife noise.

Two-Front War

The greatest achievement of the Drak'vorr resistance was the speed at which it understood the kind of conflict they were fighting; this was a planetary-scale campaign of attrition. While the Imperial High Command initially dismissed the Drak'vorr as "primitive," the physical durability of the species and their rapid adaptation to asymmetric warfare forced the Empire into a costly meat-grinder that nearly bankrupted the military’s political capital.

While countless rebel cells engaged the Empire's military across the planet; sympathetic Drak'vorr in Imperial strongholds engaged in subtle, long-term sabotage. Imperial logistical shipments were routinely misrouted, imperial buildings were often built well below standards, and intelligence about imperial troop movements flowed like a hose back to the Underground.

In close-quarters urban and subterranean combat, of which the rebels were keen to find themselves in, a single Drak'vorr was capable of overpowering an entire squad of armored Imperial infantry through sheer brute force and durability. By 2703, large quantities of high-tech imperial equipment had fallen into the hands of the Underground, levelling much of the advantage the Empire had against them.

The high point of the resistance occurred in 2704, during a simultaneous attack on the three completed military spires. The Battle of the Pillars was a key victory for the rebellion, for relatively little military investment. Utilizing a suicide team, the Drak'vorr managed to carry industrial explosives into the reactor systems of two of the three towers. The subsequent chain reaction caused the buildings to collapse under their own weight, crippling the Imperial logistics and command structure.

The destruction of military command towers was the deadliest day for the Solar Empire in the entire insurgency, and the deadliest engagement fought by Equestrians since the end of the Great War two and a half centuries prior. By the end of the war, the Empire would suffer over 200,000 killed/missing in action or critically wounded.

Imperial Victory

Seeking to break the stalemate, Imperial High Command shifted strategy; traditional warfare and counter-insurgency operations gave way to the total destruction of the Drak'vorr society. If the empire could not face the Underground in their subterranean hideaways, they would force the war into their own terms.

The Empire engaged in aforced relocation of entire Drak'vorr populations into "Strategic Hamlets." Anyone found outside these zones was labeled a combatant and targeted from orbit, starving the resistance of their support network. In a dark irony, the Empire realized that the very strength the Drak'vorr used to kill imperial soldiers could be used against them. Using powerful mind control magics, unicorn combat mages forced squads of captured rebels to fight their former comrades.

The war ended in 2709 (39 IE not with a grand treaty, but with the utter exhaustion of the Drak'vorr. Millions of Drak'vorr casualites, most of them civilians lost to scorched earth famines, broke the back of the insurgency. There was no formal surrender of the Underground; they either starved, were executed, or quietly reintegrated back into their people's broken society.

Imperialization

As the resistance entered its final, desperate years, the Empire ceased all pretenses of civilian governance. The planet was placed under total military rule, turning the entire Drak'vorr population into a logistical component of the Imperial war machine. Every Drak'vorr over of working was assigned a labor quota. Failure to meet the quota resulted in the "ration-docking" of their entire familial unit. The extreme focus on Drakan's military resource economy proved beneficial to the Empire's war machine. By 2708, Drakan provided 40% of the structural alloys used in the Imperial Phoenix-Class cruisers.

The most transformative—and controversial—aspect of post-war Drakan was the formalization of the Drak'vorr Legion. Having already laid the groundwork in the form of dogmatic state schools, the High Command saw promise in a force of absolutely loyal and supremely strong shock troops. As part of the terms that established their rights as Imperial citizens, every Drak'vorr community was required to provide 10% of its male offspring to the Imperial military. These children were taken to orbital stations, away from the influence of their culture. Through a combination of chemical conditioning and rigorous training, their natural protective instincts were redirected toward Empress Celestia.

The Empire understood that the resistance was fueled by Drak'vorr history and oral tradition. Following the end of armed resistance, a program that preached "cultural harmony" was enacted. Most native Drak'vorr dialects were classified as "archaic and inefficient." All public signage, education, and legal proceedings were conducted in a single, approved Drak'vorr dialect. Veneration of Empress Celestia was inserted into the ancient religious traditions of the planet, usually with her taking the place as an aspect of their god and a savior who delivered the Drak'vorr from the "Great Dying".

Imperial Tribunals on Drakan

Beginning in 40 IE (2710), the Imperial Tribunals on Drakan were a series of landmark civilian-led civilian-led criminal proceedings that resulted in the systematic purge of the Imperial High Command. While ostensibly convened to address rights violations and "Crimes Against Sentience" during the pacification of Drakan, the Tribunals are now widely recognized as the pivotal moment when the Empress Celestia transitioned from a constitutional figurehead to an absolute autocrat.

Background and Context

By 39 IE, Imperial authority over the Thraskir system had been fully enforced, but at a staggering cost. The decade long resistance movement had resulted in over 200,000 Imperial casualties (KIA, WIA, or critically wounded) and millions of Drak'vorr. The military "Old Guard," a faction of ultra-nationalist generals and admiral who are believed to have orchestrated the rise Solar Empire of following the 1st Battle of Nekmer, had used the conflict to justify a massive expansion of their political influence, often bypassing civilian oversight.

These ultra-nationalist had also been the impetuous behind the initial occupation of Drakan. The relationship between Empress Celestia and the High Command reached a breaking point in 39 IE. Historical consensus suggests the Empress viewed the military's growing independence as a direct threat to the stability of the Throne.

In early 40 IE, the Empress’s administration leaked the Brightstar Dossiers to the public via her loyal propaganda arm. These documents contained unredacted accounts of the brutality on Drakan, including the illegal use of chemical defoliants and the summary execution of Drak'vorr non-combatants. Crucially, the leaks highlighted the High Command's "strategic incompetence," suggesting that thousands of Imperial soldiers had died due to the vanity of the officer class. This sparked widespread civil unrest in Manehattan—Equus' most alien-diverse city—and across the core worlds, shifting public sentiment from patriotic fervor to a demand for military accountability.

Tribunal Proceedings

Breaking with the tradition of military self-policing, the Empress invoked the her emergency powers (never having been revoked following the 1st Battle of Nekmer), placing the military under the jurisdiction of the civilian High Court.

14 High Admirals and 32 Generals faced charges of "Crimes Against Sentience." A novel legal concept introduced by the Ministry of Justice's prosecutors, it defined the Drak'vorr not as mere wartime enemies, but as "protected subjects of the Empress" who had been failed by their military administrators. The "Old Guard" argued they were acting under the implicit mandate of the state to secure the Empire's borders. Eventually, 41 of the 46 defendants were found guilty of High Treason and Malignant Negligence. Empress Celestia maintained a public image of "the grieving mother of the nation" throughout the trials. She frequently visited the families of soldiers killed on Drakan, framing the executions of the generals as a necessary "cleansing" of the Imperial spirit.

On the 100th day of the Tribunals, the 15 highest-ranking officers were executed via painless disentigration in a ceremony broadcast to every world and space-station in the Empire. The remaining officers were stripped of their titles and replaced by a cadre of younger officers that hard largely been uninvolved with the occupation. A few of the newly promoted planetary generals had served as lesser officers on Drakan, hand-picked due to their loyalty to the Empress over the former military leadership.

Aftermath

The tribunals were lauded at the time as a victory for civilian ethics and "moral governance." Even outside powers, such as the USC, that had issued condemnations of the initial occupation of Drakan praised the Imperial government for the handling of its own military. However, modern historians emphasize the profound irony of the Imperial Tribunals, noting that the practical outcomes were starkly different from the stated goals.

The removal of the military's old guard eliminated the last institutional check on the Empress' power, paving the way for two and a half centuries of Imperial abuses. Case in point, despite the "Crimes Against Sentience" verdict, the Empress did not liberate the Drak'vorr. Instead, the janissary programs and forced labor quotas were merely reorganized under the Department of Alien Affairs, becoming more efficient and less visible.

By her own testimony, the tribunals were a line that had been crossed to Princess Luna, who withdrew from public affairs shortly after the final executions. Realizing that the tribunals were a weaponization of justice rather than a pursuit of it, Luna instead devoted herself to the management of her executive governorship rather than imperial policy work; and eventually laying the groundwork for her rebellion against her sister.


The Drakan Tribunals were the most successful theater in the history of the Empire. In a single stroke, Celestia assassinated her rivals, tricked her citizens, and shackled the military to her will—all while wearing the face of a crying mother.

- Luna, Tellings of a Dark Time
